data.title: Nidec Shares Rise 2.5% After 1Q Preliminary Net Profit Beat Consensus
Tokyo Core Inflation Slows, But Stays Above BOJ Target
data.description:
Core consumer prices in Tokyo’s Ku-area rose 2.9% year-on-year in July 2025, easing for the second straight month and falling just below market expectations of 3%.

Despite the slowdown, inflation remains well above the Bank of Japan’s 2% target, keeping expectations alive for another rate hike later this year.

The BOJ will meet next week to decide on monetary policy, with markets anticipating an upward revision to its inflation forecast for the current fiscal year in its quarterly outlook.

Still, growth concerns persist. In May, the central bank lowered its GDP projections due to the drag from higher US tariffs, complicating the path for future rate increases.

However, a recently signed US-Japan trade deal, which includes a 15% tariff on Japanese exports to the US, has reduced some of the uncertainty surrounding the economic outlook.

data.title: JGB Futures Consolidate; Cooling Tokyo Inflation May Support — Market Talk
data.description: JGB futures consolidate in the morning Tokyo session, but may be supported by cooling inflation in the Tokyo metropolitan area. Data released earlier showed core consumer prices in Tokyo, excluding fresh food, rose 2.9% from a year earlier in July. That was down from June's 3.1% increase. The Tokyo readings, considered an early indicator of nationwide trends, come ahead of the Bank of Japan's meeting next week, where the central bank is expected to leave monetary policy unchanged. The 10-year JGB futures are 0.03 yen higher at 137.54 yen. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Intel earnings tell two stories, as revenue impresses but losses pile up from restructuring
data.description:
By Britney Nguyen

The chip maker's stock was moving lower after mixed results and guidance

Intel Corp. brought in more revenue last quarter than Wall Street expected, even as the company racked up restructuring charges that are hitting profits.

The chip maker reported mixed results for the June quarter on Thursday, with total revenue of $12.9 billion that was flat from the previous year but above FactSet analysts' consensus for $12 billion. However, Intel also posted an adjusted loss of 10 cents per share, whereas analysts tracked by FactSet had been modeling only 1 cent in adjusted earnings per share.

Intel's stock (INTC) was down about 3% in Thursday's extended session following the earnings call, after initially showing gains.

The company said it "has completed the majority of the planned headcount actions" from cutting its workforce by about 15%, resulting in $1.9 billion in restructuring charges in the second quarter that weren't included in its adjusted results. It posted a $2.9 billion GAAP loss.

Intel also incurred $800 million of impairment charges and $200 million in "one-time period costs" that hit adjusted earnings per share by 20 cents.

"Our operating performance demonstrates the initial progress we are making to improve our execution and drive greater efficiency," Chief Executive Lip-Bu Tan said in a statement. "We are laser-focused on strengthening our core product portfolio and our AI road map to better serve customers."

Intel's client computing segment saw revenue of $7.9 billion in the second quarter, while revenue for its data-center and AI business came in at $3.9 billion - above Wall Street's calls for $7.4 billion and $3.6 billion, respectively, according to FactSet.

The company set its third-quarter revenue guidance at between $12.6 billion and $13.6 billion, ahead of the $12.7 billion FactSet consensus at the midpoint. But the bottom-line view came up short, with Intel expecting to break even on adjusted earnings per share, while analysts were expecting a 4-cent profit.

Earlier this week, Bernstein analysts said investors would likely be less focused on Intel's earnings results, as they'd instead be looking for what the company shares about its strategy for the future. Under Tan, who became CEO in March, Intel is facing questions of what will happen to its foundry business and how its chip technology is advancing as it falls further behind in the artificial-intelligence race.

Tan said in the statement Thursday that the company is working on building "a more financially disciplined foundry." Although it will "take time," he said the company sees "clear opportunities to enhance our competitive position, improve our profitability and create long-term shareholder value."

See more: Intel's upcoming earnings have this analyst wondering if the numbers actually matter

The Bernstein team, led by Stacy Rasgon, said Intel's PC business "may be OK for now on the back of tariff dynamics." But they said they "doubt anyone is going to buy the stock" for that potential because "the pull-forward drivers seem almost certain at this point."

Bernstein also said structural headwinds to Intel's business "seem to be getting worse than better." The analysts thought the "broader questions around strategy and where they go from here seem much more important than whether they beat or miss the quarter."

In a Wednesday note to clients, BofA Securities analysts said that despite headwinds to Intel's business and competitiveness, they "view a faster ramp of 18A" - referring to the chip-manufacturing process that the company spent billions of dollars developing under previous CEO Pat Gelsinger.

Tan said on the company's earnings call that Intel is continuing to make progress on yields and performance targets for 18A products, and that it will be the foundation of at least the next three generations of the company's client and server products. The 18A family is also important, Tan said, as Intel advances its work for the U.S. government and initial committed customers.

"Up to and through Intel 18A, we could generate a reasonable return on our investments with only Intel products," Tan said. "The increase in capital costs at Intel 14A make it clear that we need both Intel products and a meaningful external customer to drive acceptable returns on our deployed capital."

Intel 14A is the next-generation node after 18A.

The BofA team also said that Intel's "upcoming enterprise refresh cycle could help drive near-term upside, off the current depressed outlook." The analysts maintained a neutral rating on Intel's stock, pointing to its "turnaround potentials." However, that is "offset" by continued competition with Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD) and Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) in the market for central processing units, the analysts noted, as well as its lacking a portfolio of AI accelerators.

Tan said on the earnings call that he has implemented a policy where he "personally" reviews and approves "every major chip design" before tape out, or the final chip-design stage before it is sent to a foundry to be manufactured.

Intel will "unfold our AI strategy in the months to come," Tan said on the earnings call, in response to an analyst question, adding that the company is looking to focus on the need for AI inferencing and agentic AI.

-Britney Nguyen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Recover Investment Losses: Levi & Korsinsky Files Class Action Against Vestis Corporation (VSTS)
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESS Newswire / July 24, 2025 / If you suffered a loss on your Vestis Corporation investment and want to learn about a potential recovery under the federal securities laws, follow the link below for more information:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/vestis-corporation-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=158065&wire=1&utm_campaign=24

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq. via email at jlevi@levikorsinsky.com or call (212) 363-7500 to speak to our team of experienced shareholder advocates.

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against Vestis Corporation that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between May 2, 2024 and May 6, 2025.

CASE DETAILS: According to the complaint, defendants provided overwhelmingly positive statements to investors while, at the same time, disseminating materially false and misleading statements and/or concealing material adverse facts concerning the true state of Vestis' ability to grow its business; notably that Vestis would be unable to execute on planned strategic initiatives to drive purported improvements to the customer experience and its onboarding efforts in order to drive new customer growth, increased customer retention, and increased revenue from existing customers.

On May 7, 2025, Vestis announced its financial results for the second quarter of fiscal 2025, withdrew its revenue and growth guidance for the full fiscal year 2025, and provided guidance for the third quarter of fiscal 2025 that fell significantly below market expectations. The Company attributed its poor results partially to "lost business in excess of new business," but primarily on "lower adds over stops, which is how we describe volume changes with our existing customers." The Company attributed its decision to pull full-year guidance and provide disappointing third quarter targets to the "increasingly uncertain macro environment."

Following this news, the price of Vestis' common stock declined dramatically. From a closing market price of $8.71 per share on May 6, 2025, Vestis' stock price fell to $5.44 per share on May 7, 2025, a decline of about 37.54% in the span of just a single day.

data.title: Step One Target Price Cut 33% to A$1.50/Share by Morgans>STP.AU
Oil Rises Amid Hopes for U.S.-EU Trade Agreement — Market Talk
data.description: Oil rises in the early Asian trade. U.S. and EU officials look like they are converging on a possible deal, after the White House's agreement with Tokyo earlier in the week. European officials expect a 15% tariff on most EU exports to the U.S., in line with the Japan deal. This news has lifted confidence that a worst-case scenario of a 30% tariff is avoidable, ANZ Research analysts say in a research report. Positive sentiment has also been supported by technical-related buying, the analysts add. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are 0.3% higher at $66.21/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.3% higher at $69.36/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2006 ET - Oil rises in the early Asian trade. U.S. and EU officials look like they are converging on a possible deal, after the White House's agreement with Tokyo earlier in the week. European officials expect a 15% tariff on most EU exports to the U.S., in line with the Japan deal. This news has lifted confidence that a worst-case scenario of a 30% tariff is avoidable, ANZ Research analysts say in a research report. Positive sentiment has also been supported by technical-related buying, the analysts add. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are 0.3% higher at $66.21/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.3% higher at $69.36/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2000 ET - Whitehaven recorded a good cost performance for FY25, says Citi analyst Paul McTaggart, after what he calls a "robust" 4Q result from the Australian coal miner. McTaggart says Whitehaven's 4Q saleable production was 9% higher than Citi expected. Cost guidance for FY26 will come next month with Whitehaven's FY fiscal results, he notes. Citi has a buy rating on the stock, with a target price of A$7.00. Whitehaven ended Thursday at A$7.03/share. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1525 ET - U.S. natural gas futures eke out a small gain after the EIA reports a below-normal inventory build of 23 Bcf for last week, leaving inventories at 3,075 Bcf or 171 Bcf above the five-year average. "The market was up modestly heading into the report after finding a modicum of support overnight, but the smaller-than-expected build has so far not added any significant bullish sentiment back into the fray," says Andy Huenefeld of Pinebrook Energy Advisors in a note. "Intense heat over the coming week should continue to limit storage builds for the next two reports, but a shift to a milder solution in the East near the end of the 10-day period should help to alleviate demand from there." Nymex natural gas settles up 0.6% at $3.094/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1504 ET - Oil rises for the first time in five sessions with help from expectations of the U.S. reaching a trade deal with the EU, while continuing to trade in a tight range. "The price action indicates market participants are getting increasingly sanguine about the outlook for oil markets, despite the accelerated phase-out of additional voluntary adjustments by OPEC+," Amarpreet Singh of Barclays says in a note. Oil stocks in the U.S., refining cracks and high-frequency trade flows continue to signal robust demand, "while production in the U.S. seems to be rolling over," he adds. "Based on the weekly estimates from the EIA and corporate commentary, we think risks to our U.S. crude oil production forecasts are skewed lower." WTI settles up 1.2% at $66.03 a barrel, and Brent rises 1% to $69.18 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1426 ET - Oil futures are gaining but off earlier highs after WSJ reports that the Trump administration plans to allow Chevron to again pump oil in Venezuela, citing people familiar with the plan. The U.S. sources heavy crude oil from Venezuela and other countries such as Canada and Mexico. The U.S. imported 170,000 barrels a day of Venezuelan oil in April before Chevron's license expired, according to EIA data. That was down from almost 300,000 b/d in December and January. WTI is up 0.5% at $65.58 a barrel, and Brent is up 0.3% at $68.72.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1356 ET - The cost to ship grains from a port in the U.S. Gulf to Japan reached the highest point since last year, reflecting robust export sales globally. According to the USDA's Grain Transportation report, the rate for shipping a metric ton of grain from the Gulf to Japan was $52.75 as of July 17. That's the highest rate since last October and up $4 from the previous week--the biggest uptick in nearly 2 years, says the USDA. President Trump announced a trade deal with Japan this week, which includes many agriculture-centric provisions. CBOT grains are mostly lower in today's session, with soybeans down 0.1% and wheat off 0.3% while corn rises 0.4%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1106 ET - Natural gas stored underground in the U.S. increased by less than usual last week, reducing the surplus over the five-year average. Gas inventories rose by 23 billion cubic feet to 3,075 Bcf, the EIA reports. The storage injection was smaller than the 2020-2024 average for the week of 30 Bcf, and below the 31 Bcf estimate in a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts. Nymex natural gas is up 1.3% at $3.117/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1016 ET - U.S. natural gas futures gain some ground with the market awaiting weekly inventory data from the EIA, which is expected to show a storage injection in line with the five-year average. Analysts in a Wall Street Journal survey predict an inventory build of 31 Bcf to 3,083 Bcf. "With August at a critical technical inflection point, a surprise in either direction could spur a relief rally or a test of $3.00/mmBtu," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. Nymex natural gas is up 2.2% at $3.143/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0957 ET - Oil futures gain ground with signs of U.S. progress toward trade deals taking some of the tariff-related pessimism out of the market. Yesterday's U.S. inventory data showing a 3.2 million barrel crude stock draw is also supportive of prices, Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. Technically September WTI "remains in a sideways bullish type of structure," he says, with major resistance at $66.11 and support at $64.22. WTI is up 0.9% at $65.85 a barrel and Brent is 0.7% higher at $69.01. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0715 ET - Oil prices continue to trade higher in midday trading, as progress in tariff negotiations between the Trump's administration and the EU ease market concerns over the global economic outlook. "The main theme overnight was risk-on," Commerzbank Research's Tommy Wu and Moses Lim say. "The latest trade developments spurred optimism among investors that the U.S. could reach more trade deals with its trading partners before the impending deadline on August 1." Prices also find support from signs of robust summer fuel demand in the U.S. and unresolved geopolitical tensions, with a third round of talks between Russia and Ukraine showing no meaningful progress toward ending the war. Brent crude rises 0.7% to $68.97 a barrel, while WTI is up 0.8% to $65.81 a barrel. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0640 ET - While Centrica posted in-line results and reiterated its guidance, consensus estimates have yet to appropriately incorporate the U.K. energy company's sale of the Cygnus gas field, JPMorgan analysts say in a note. While the consensus for earnings per share of 12.6 pence appears to be consistent with company guidance, JPM continues to expect 11.8 pence earnings per share. Centrica swinging to an overall loss highlights the challenging backdrop of the country's energy sector, which has read-across for peers operating in the U.K., such as E.ON and RWE, they say. Shares trade 1.6% higher at 161.25 pence. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

0602 ET - European natural-gas prices edge lower in early trading, hovering around 33 euros a megawatt hour as EU member states continue to replenish storage facilities. "Europe has boosted LNG imports this year after ending winter with reserves lower than usual," analysts at ANZ Research say. "However, lower demand from China, where imports fell almost 20% in the first half of the year, has increased the availability of cargoes on the spot market." EU gas inventories are now more than 65% full, putting the bloc on track to reach the 80% target by Nov. 1, but remain below last year's levels and the five-year average, according to market watchers. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

First Savings Financial Group Inc reports results for the quarter ended June 30 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• First Savings Financial Group Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 81 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended June 30, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 60 cents. The mean expectation of three analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 74 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 69 cents to 80 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 15.1% to $16.73 million from a year ago; analysts expected $16.94 million.

  • First Savings Financial Group Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 88 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $6.17 million.

  • First Savings Financial Group Inc shares had risen by 4.1% this quarter and lost 1.9% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 7.8% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 3 "strong buy" or "buy," no "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell."

data.title: US lifts sanctions on Myanmar junta allies after general praises Trump
data.description:
The United States on Thursday lifted sanctions designations on several allies of Myanmar's ruling generals, two weeks after the head of the ruling junta praised President Donald Trump and called for an easing of sanctions in a letter responding to a tariff warning.

A notice from the U.S. Treasury Department said KT Services & Logistics and its founder, Jonathan Myo Kyaw Thaung; the MCM Group and its owner Aung Hlaing Oo; and Suntac Technologies and its owner Sit Taing Aung; and another individual, Tin Latt Min, were being removed from the U.S. sanctions list.

KT Services & Logistics and Jonathan Myo Kyaw Thaung were added to the sanctions list in January 2022 under the Biden administration in a step timed to mark the first anniversary of the military seizure of power in Myanmar that plunged the country into chaos.

Sit Taing Aung and Aung Hlaing Oo were placed on the sanctions list the same year for operating in Myanmar's defense sector. Tin Latt Min, identified as another close associate of the military rulers, was placed on the list in 2024 to mark the third anniversary of the coup.

Treasury did not explain the reason for the move, and the White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

On July 11, Myanmar's ruling military general, Min Aung Hlaing, asked Trump in a letter for a reduction in the 40% tariff rate on his country's exports to the U.S. and said he was ready to send a negotiation team to Washington if needed.

"The senior general acknowledged the president's strong leadership in guiding his country towards national prosperity with the spirit of a true patriot," state media said at the time.

In his response to a letter from Trump notifying Myanmar of the tariff to take effect on August 1, Min Aung Hlaing proposed a reduced rate of 10% to 20%, with Myanmar slashing its levy on U.S. imports to a range of zero to 10%.

Min Aung Hlaing also asked Trump "to reconsider easing and lifting the economic sanctions imposed on Myanmar, as they hinder the shared interests and prosperity of both countries and their peoples."

Myanmar is one of the world's main sources of sought-after rare earth minerals used in high-tech defense and consumer applications. Securing supplies of the minerals is a major focus for the Trump administration in its strategic competition with China, which is responsible for 90% of rare earth processing capacity.

Most of Myanmar's rare earth mines are in areas controlled by the Kachin Independence Army (KIA), an ethnic group fighting the junta, and are processed in China.

data.title: SHAREHOLDER ACTION REMINDER: Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP Investigates Claims on Behalf of Investors of Fortrea Holdings
data.description:
Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP Securities Litigation Partner James (Josh) Wilson Encourages Investors Who Suffered Losses In Fortrea To Contact Him Directly To Discuss Their Options

If you purchased or acquired securites in Fortrea between July 3, 2023 and February 28, 2025 and would like to discuss your legal rights, call Faruqi & Faruqi partner Josh Wilson directly at 877-247-4292 or 212-983-9330 (Ext. 1310).

[You may also click here for additional information]

New York, New York--(Newsfile Corp. - July 24, 2025) - Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP, a leading national securities law firm, is investigating potential claims against Fortrea Holdings Inc. ("Fortrea" or the "Company") and reminds investors of the August 1, 2025 deadline to seek the role of lead plaintiff in a federal securities class action that has been filed against the Company.

Faruqi & Faruqi is a leading national securities law firm with offices in New York, Pennsylvania, California and Georgia. The firm has recovered hundreds of millions of dollars for investors since its founding in 1995. See www.faruqilaw.com.

As detailed below, the complaint alleges that the Company and its executives violated federal securities laws by making false and/or misleading statements and/or failing to disclose that: (1) Fortrea overestimated the amount of revenue the Pre-Spin Projects were likely to contribute to the Company's 2025 earnings; (2) Fortrea overstated the cost savings it would likely achieve by exiting the TSAs; (3) as a result, the Company's previously announced EBITDA targets for 2025 were inflated; (4) accordingly, the viability of the Company's post-Spin-Off business model, as well as its business and/or financial prospects, were overstated; and (5) as a result, the Company's public statements were materially false and misleading at all relevant times.

On September 25, 2024, the investment bank Jefferies published a report (the "Jefferies Report") downgrading Fortrea from buy to hold. Among other things, the Jefferies Report cited perceived weaknesses in the Company's business model as a CRO amid pressure on biotechnology funding and suggested that the cost savings Fortrea expects to achieve by exiting the TSAs are "not as material as [o]ne [m]ight think," stating that "IT infrastructure costs to exit the TSAs are already non-GAAPed out of adjusted EBITDA. Thus, once TSAs are exited, Fortrea will just be replacing TSA costs with internal operating costs."

On this news, Fortrea's stock price fell $2.73 per share, or 12.29%, to close at $19.48 per share on September 25, 2024.

Then, on December 6, 2024, market analyst Baird Equity Research ("Baird") downgraded Fortrea to neutral from outperform after the Company abruptly cancelled two scheduled conferences. A Baird analyst said that "given our ongoing concerns around the sector, Fortrea's choppy history post spin, and lack of clarity on the abrupt communications course change, we cannot recommend an actionable investment (buy or sell)[.]"

On this news, Fortrea's stock price fell $1.90 per share, or 8.06%, to close at $21.67 per share on December 6, 2024.

Finally, on March 3, 2025, Fortrea announced its fourth quarter and full year 2024 financial results, disclosing that its "targeted revenue and adjusted EBITDA trajectories for 2025 [were] not in line with [its] prior expectations." Specifically, in an earnings call held that same day, Fortrea revealed that the Company's Pre-Spin projects are "late in their life cycle [and] have less revenue and less profitability than expected for 2025" and that "post-spin work is not coming on fast enough to offset the pre-spin contract economics." The Company also said this "older versus newer mix issue will continue to negatively impact Fortrea's financial performance during 2025."

On this news, Fortrea's stock price fell $3.47 per share, or 25.05%, to close at $10.38 per share on March 3, 2025.

The court-appointed lead plaintiff is the investor with the largest financial interest in the relief sought by the class who is adequate and typical of class members who directs and oversees the litigation on behalf of the putative class. Any member of the putative class may move the Court to serve as lead plaintiff through counsel of their choice, or may choose to do nothing and remain an absent class member. Your ability to share in any recovery is not affected by the decision to serve as a lead plaintiff or not.

Faruqi & Faruqi, LLP also encourages anyone with information regarding Fortrea's conduct to contact the firm, including whistleblowers, former employees, shareholders and others.

To learn more about the Fortrea class action, go to www.faruqilaw.com/FTRE or call Faruqi & Faruqi partner Josh Wilson directly at 877-247-4292 or 212-983-9330 (Ext. 1310).

Follow us for updates on LinkedIn, on X, or on Facebook.

data.title: Baking giant Bimbo vows to cut artificial colorings by end-2026
data.description:
Grupo Bimbo , one of the world's largest breadmakers, said on Thursday it would cut artificial colorings from all of its products by the end of next year, as consumers turn more health-conscious when shopping for staple foods.

Shorter term, executives at the Mexico City-based company said Bimbo's entire bread bun and breakfast range would by the end of this year have a positive nutrition - or a so-called health star rating - of at least 3.5 stars, as it looks to simplify recipes and boost nutrition by 2030.

WHY IT'S IMPORTANT

Scientists have linked synthetic dyes such as Red 40 and Yellow 5 to behavioral challenges, allergies and respiratory issues in children and other vulnerable consumers, and some major regulators have ordered usage caps and label warnings.

In parts of the U.S., some activists have pushed for bans on synthetic dyes particularly in food destined for school meals.

BY THE NUMBERS

Bimbo estimates it is the largest single supplier of baked goods worldwide, with a close to 4% global market share of a $641 billion industry. It sells thousands of well-known staple products worldwide such as sliced bread and packaged snacks.

Last year, these brought in $22 billion in sales. Executives said they did not expect a major cost impact from cutting artificial colorings.

KEY QUOTES

"By the end of 2026, we will have removed artificial colors from all our portfolio and by 2030 we're going to ensure that 100% of our baked goods and snacks will be made with simple, natural recipes," Bimbo Chief Financial Officer Diego Gaxiola told analysts in a call.

"We're seeing that for younger consumers functional benefits are clearly important. It's not a fad, it's a trend."

data.title: Nikkei Slips 0.3%, Dragged by Auto Stocks — Market Talk
data.description:
2009 ET - Japanese stocks are lower as initial enthusiasm over the U.S. trade deal subsides following two days of sharp gains. Carmakers are leading declines. Subaru is down 3.3% and Mazda Motor is 3.3% lower. USD/JPY is at 147.22, compared with 146.22 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are closely watching corporate earnings, with Fanuc scheduled to announce its quarterly results later in the day. The Nikkei Stock Average is down 0.3% at 41691.09. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

1947 ET - Japanese stocks may decline as initial enthusiasm over the U.S. trade deal subsides following two days of sharp gains. Nikkei futures are down 0.5% at 41625 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 147.10, compared with 146.22 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on corporate earnings, with Fanuc set to report its results later in the day. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 1.6% to 41826.34 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1944 ET - South Korea's anticipated 2H gross domestic product growth recovery will likely be driven by consumption, ING economist Min Joo Kang writes in a note. The primary driver to stimulate consumption should be the government's cash handout program from this week through November, Kang says. She expects consumer spending to also be boosted by the Korean equity market which has performed well. The stock benchmark Kospi is up 33% this year. Recent improvement in consumer sentiment also supports the expected consumption recovery, she adds. Official data earlier this week showed that the country's 2Q GDP rebounded at a stronger-than-expected pace thanks to exports and private spending. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

1538 ET - U.S. labor data supports a long Fed hold and intensifies a Treasury selloff, pushing yields to a second consecutive rising session. Recent trade deals have been fueling risk-taking, too.Data show layoffs remain contained, removing a potential reason for the Fed to cut interest rates. President Trump, however, keeps calling for fast monetary easing, making markets suspect a more dovish stance lies ahead. June new-home sales grow less than expected, reversing May's decline. Durable goods orders, due tomorrow, are expected to contract 11.1% in a WSJ survey, following May's 16.4% expansion. The 10-year rises 0.021 percentage point to 4.408% and the two-year adds 0.041 p.p. to 3.924%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1457 ET - The CD Howe Institute's monetary-policy committee says the Bank of Canada should leave its policy interest rate unchanged next week at 2.75% but follow that up with a cut in September. The Toronto-based committee is made up of economists from academia and the private sector, and their recommendation represents the median vote of economists in attendance. U.S. tariffs and U.S.-Canada negotiations on a tariff resolution dominated the discussions, the CD Howe think says in a summary of deliberations. Members expressed surprise about underlying economic resiliency, although adding that cracks are starting to surface. Still, a surprisingly strong jobs report for July and elevated core CPI were the key factors persuading committee members to recommend a BOC hold. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1354 ET - Another index gauging Canadian consumer sentiment is on the upswing for July. The Conference Board of Canada's consumer-confidence index rose in July to 62.7. This marks a fourth-straight month-over-month increase after hitting its lowest level on record in March, at 44.2. The board notes that despite the increases, the index reading remains below year-ago levels. Still, the reading suggests some resiliency among Canadian households. The index' climb comes on the same day that Statistics Canada reports that it estimates retail sales likely jumped 1.6% in June, following a 1.1% drop in May. The board's confidence-index summary says the increase is tied to more favorable macroeconomic signals, such as easing price pressures and labor-market gains. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1139 ET - The recovery in Canadian retail sales last month could yet prove short lived. After households reined in spending in May, with retail sales down 1.1% on-month amid elevated trade policy uncertainty and a softening labor market, the advance estimate points to a 1.6% rise in sales in June. Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport says momentum in consumer spending will continue to fade as the downturn from the trade war broadens to the household sector. He reckons that unless a trade deal is reached to significantly reduce U.S.-Canada tariffs by Aug. 1, when new U.S. tariffs are set to come into effect, "households will continue to tighten their purse strings as job losses and higher prices from tariffs squeeze disposable income." (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1050 ET - Initial jobless claims continued a declining trend last week, but the labor market might be weakening more than the report suggests, writes Oliver Allen of Pantheon Macro. In particular, summer maintenance shutdowns at car factories make it hard to see through seasonal adjustments in jobless-claims figures this time of year, he notes. "We would caution against concluding that the further drop in initial claims to their lowest level since April sounds the all-clear on the labor market," he writes. That's not to say there's a job-market emergency afoot--but Allen suggests that elevated continuing-claims data indicate a gradual but persistent weakening. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1022 ET - Doubts about the future of the Fed's leadership weaken the dollar, Bank of America's Alex Cohen writes. The uncertainty has "arguably impacted the USD more than other assets so far" and is likely keeping the dollar from rallying, Cohen says. The issue isn't going away. "Even if a more conventional leadership transition occurs, the market is starting to contemplate a world with a less restrictive Fed." President Trump is keeping pressure on Chair Powell to lower rates, including a planned to tour of Fed headquarters this afternoon. The WSJ Dollar Index pares early gains and is now flat. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1019 ET - The Los Angeles wildfires in January impacted $51.7 billion worth of homes--or roughly 11,000 residential properties--according to Redfin. The report analyzes data from the Los Angeles City Council. The council shared a list of 11,125 residential parcels in the city of Los Angeles that were inspected by the Los Angeles Department of Building and Safety after the wildfires. Many, but not all, were completely destroyed by the fires. Most homes in this analysis were impacted by the Palisades Fire. This analysis excludes the impact of the Eaton Fire, which impacted the Los Angeles suburb of Altadena, meaning the total value of homes destroyed in the January fires likely far exceeds $51.7 billion. Redfin says the typical impacted home had a value of $3.7 million prior to the fires. Nearly 100 properties had a value of over $20 million prior to the fires. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1000 ET - The European Central Bank is focused on protecting its currency and delivering a digital euro rather than the implications of advances in U.S. cryptocurrency legislation, ECB President Christine Lagarde said Thursday. President Trump last week signed legislation that provides a regulatory framework for stablecoins, a type of cryptocurrency pegged to another asset like the dollar. Asked about the implications of stablecoins for monetary policy at a press conference, Lagarde said the ECB is mindful of what's happening elsewhere but focused on "protecting and preserving our currency." The ECB aims to deliver a digital euro as soon as possible. However it won't happen tomorrow, cash is always there and banks will be involved, she said. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0958 ET - Money markets no longer fully price in a 25-basis-point interest-rate cut by the European Central Bank in 2025 after the ECB's decision to leave interest rates on hold and after President Christine Lagarde's press conference. Prior to the ECB meeting, markets had priced in a nearly 90% chance of a rate cut in December, according to LSEG data. They now price in only a 68% chance of this scenario happening. Markets nevertheless see a high probability of a rate cut in March or April next year, LSEG data show. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2009 ET - Japanese stocks are lower as initial enthusiasm over the U.S. trade deal subsides following two days of sharp gains. Carmakers are leading declines. Subaru is down 3.3% and Mazda Motor is 3.3% lower. USD/JPY is at 147.22, compared with 146.22 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are closely watching corporate earnings, with Fanuc scheduled to announce its quarterly results later in the day. The Nikkei Stock Average is down 0.3% at 41691.09. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

2000 ET - Whitehaven recorded a good cost performance for FY25, says Citi analyst Paul McTaggart, after what he calls a "robust" 4Q result from the Australian coal miner. McTaggart says Whitehaven's 4Q saleable production was 9% higher than Citi expected. Cost guidance for FY26 will come next month with Whitehaven's FY fiscal results, he notes. Citi has a buy rating on the stock, with a target price of A$7.00. Whitehaven ended Thursday at A$7.03/share. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1947 ET - Japanese stocks may decline as initial enthusiasm over the U.S. trade deal subsides following two days of sharp gains. Nikkei futures are down 0.5% at 41625 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 147.10, compared with 146.22 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on corporate earnings, with Fanuc set to report its results later in the day. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 1.6% to 41826.34 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1910 ET [Dow Jones]--Australia's S&P/ASX 200 seems on course for a weekly decline, with local stock futures pointing to a lower open for the benchmark index. ASX futures are down by almost 0.5% ahead of Friday's session, suggesting that the ASX 200 will add to Thursday's 0.3% slip. It is down almost 0.6% so far this week. Ahead of the open, Whitehaven Coal said annual production and sales were at the top end of guidance, with costs and capex likely lower than estimated. U.S. stocks were mixed. The S&P 500 added less than 0.1% for its fourth consecutive record, its longest such streak since December. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite gained 0.2%, also hitting a record. The DJIA slipped 0.7%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1859 ET - Boston Beer expects its new Sun Cruiser brand to continue to boost sales in its non-beer category. The vodka iced tea, which launched last summer, continues to grow volumes week-over-week as distribution expands to larger chain retailers, the company says during a call with analysts. "We believe Sun Cruiser will be the next iconic brand for the company and an important growth contributor for the 'beyond beer' category," CEO Michael Spillane says. The drink has been the company's fastest ramping product so far, he says, adding that the company plans to continue to invest in it heavily. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1747 ET - Deckers Outdoor has started raising prices on some of its shoes by around $5. The company started implementing price hikes on July 1 to offset the cost of tariffs and is planning to add more increases throughout the year. "We've been selective and staggered in our approach," CEO Stefano Caroti told analysts during a call, adding that the company doesn't want to raise prices for kids' shoes. Management is also considering hiking prices for not-yet-launched models, as well as existing ones. Caroti said the company would continue monitoring tariffs and altering its pricing plan as the situation evolves. Shares rise 8.7% after the bell. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1731 ET - Deckers Outdoors shares settled slightly after a significant 1Q earnings beat gave investors an initial thrill. Executive commentary about tariffs and persistent uncertainty in the U.S. dampened the stock's jump after the bell to around 7%, after it was up 16% at one point. The shoemaker again declined to share full-year guidance because there is still macroeconomic uncertainty. It upped its unmitigated tariff estimate to account for potentially higher duties on Vietnamese imports. More sluggish demand in the U.S. — around 3% sales growth versus 50% internationally — offset some of Deckers' gains in direct-to-consumer sales. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1656 ET - Starlink, the SpaceX satellite-internet service, experienced a network outage Thursday that prevented subscribers from getting online. SpaceX CEO Elon Musk posted an apology about the problem on X. "Service will be restored shortly," he says. "SpaceX will remedy root cause to ensure it doesn't happen again." The Starlink account on X didn't immediately identify what may have caused the outage in its own post on the social-media platform. Growth at Starlink has lifted revenue at SpaceX and helped support the company's $400 billion valuation. (micah.maidenberg@wsj.com; @MicahMaidenberg)

1605 ET - The S&P 500 and Nasdaq post fresh records as U.S. and European officials near a trade agreement. Tesla shares fall 8.2% after the EV maker reports a 16% drop in quarterly net income and said car sales continue to fall. Alphabet rises 0.9% after posting higher revenue driven by cloud growth. Chipotle drops 13% and Mattel slides 16% after reporting quarterly results. American Airlines drops 9.6% as it warns it expects to post a loss this quarter. IBM and Honeywell are the worst performers among Dow components. DJIA drops 316 points, or 0.7%, to 44693, while the S&P 500 gains 0.1% to 6363 and the Nasdaq adds 0.2% to 21057. (patrick.sullivan@wsj.com)

1510 ET - Intel may have more room to cut capital and operating expenses, according to Bank of America's Vivek Arya. Intel said in April that it would reduce its gross capital spending target by $2 billion this year to $18 billion. "However, we see potential for CEO Tan to further revise opex/capex down by 10-15%, opex toward $16bn vs. current $17bn, and capex toward $14-15bn vs. $18bn," Arya writes ahead of the company's earnings report. "While these measures could bring semblance of profit in the near-term," Arya notes concerns including "constant roadmap changes" and employee churn. Arya expects Intel's 2Q results to benefit from tariff pull-ins and a more favorable mix, but the chip company may face a tougher 2H as benefits reverse. (josh.beckerman@wsj.com)

1433 ET - O'Reilly Automotive customers are willing to pay higher prices for essential car repairs because it's cheaper than buying a new vehicle, executives say. "The strong value proposition of maintaining and repairing an existing vehicle, coupled with the high quality of vehicles, creates a very compelling incentive for our customers to prioritize their auto part spend," CEO Brad Beckham says during an analyst call. O'Reilly raised prices to mitigate new tariff costs, and increasingly complex car repairs are sending up prices, Beckham says. While there's some pullback on discretionary purchases, consumers are willing to shell out for a new alternator or battery.(katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1430 ET - O'Reilly Automotive is clocking some uncertainty among consumers despite a broader market recovery from a volatile start to this year. The car part provider says its discretionary category, which makes up a relatively small portion of its business, showed softness in 2Q. "The continued sluggishness in these categories is an indicator that consumers are still remaining cautious and conservative in how they are managing the spend in the current environment," CEO Brad Beckham says on an analyst call. He says O'Reilly's guidance is conservative to account for the possibility that consumers will pull back more in the second half of the year if prices rise. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

Weyerhaeuser Co reports results for the quarter ended June 30 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Weyerhaeuser Co reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 12 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended June 30, lower than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 21 cents. The mean expectation of eleven analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 11 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 7 cents to 18 cents per share.

  • Revenue fell 2.8% to $1.88 billion from a year ago; analysts expected $1.84 billion.

  • Weyerhaeuser Co's reported EPS for the quarter was 12 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $87 million.

  • Weyerhaeuser Co shares had risen by 1.1% this quarter and lost 7.8% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had fallen by about 48.1% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, eight analysts negatively revised earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 10 "strong buy" or "buy," 3 "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the specialized reits peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Weyerhaeuser Co is $34.00, about 24.5% above its last closing price of $25.68

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data July 25 at 12:01 a.m. UTC. ​All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated. data.title: Tokyo Inflation Signals Easing Cost Pressure Ahead of BOJ Meeting
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

TOKYO--Consumer inflation in the Tokyo metropolitan area eased slightly in July, but stubbornly high food prices continue to strain household budgets.

Core consumer prices in Tokyo, excluding fresh food, climbed 2.9% from a year earlier this month. That compared with June's 3.1% increase and matched the consensus forecast in a poll of economists by data provider Quick.

The Tokyo figures, considered an early indicator of nationwide trends, come ahead of a central bank meeting next week where policymakers will discuss inflation concerns.

While energy costs fell in July, food prices excluding fresh items increased 7.4%, compared with the 7.2% rise in June.

A survey of 195 major food makers by private research firm Teikoku Databank showed that companies planned price hikes for 2,105 products in July, about five times more than last year.

In addition to soaring raw-material prices, higher utility bills, labor costs and logistics expenses are adding to businesses' cost burden, the survey showed.

The Bank of Japan's policy board is set to release fresh price projections next week. Expectations are that it will choose to hold off on raising interest rates as it waits to see what impact U.S. tariffs will have on the Japanese economy.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com
